problems.problem: Explain why passengers lurch forward when a car suddenly stops. How does this relate to Newton's first law?

step_by_step.explanation: An object in motion stays in motion with constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force.

Passengers are moving forward with the car. When the car stops abruptly, their bodies tend to continue moving forward due to inertia. The seatbelt provides the external force to stop them; otherwise, they lurch forward.

problems.final_answer: Passengers lurch forward because their inertia resists the change in motion, consistent with Newton's first law.

problems.problem: What is the difference between dynamic equilibrium and static equilibrium?

step_by_step.explanation: Object at rest with net force = 0. Example: a book on a table.
Object moving with constant velocity with net force = 0. Example: a skydiver at terminal velocity.

problems.final_answer: Both have ΣF = 0; static equilibrium involves rest, while dynamic equilibrium involves constant velocity motion.

problems.problem: Compare two objects, one light and one heavy, at rest. Explain using inertia which one is harder to set in motion and why.

step_by_step.explanation: Inertia is the resistance to change in motion, directly proportional to mass.
The heavier object has greater mass, therefore greater inertia. It requires a larger force to achieve the same acceleration.

problems.final_answer: The heavier object is harder to set in motion because it has greater inertia.

problems.problem: A skydiver reaches terminal velocity. Describe all forces acting on the skydiver and explain why the net force is zero even though the skydiver is moving.

step_by_step.explanation: Weight (mg) downward; air resistance upward.
At terminal velocity, air resistance equals weight. Net force = 0, so acceleration = 0. The skydiver moves at constant velocity because forces are balanced.

problems.final_answer: Forces: weight down, air resistance up. Net force = 0, so constant velocity motion occurs.

problems.problem: Explain why the statement 'an object needs a continuous force to keep moving' is incorrect. Include an example from everyday life.

step_by_step.explanation: An object in motion continues at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force.

A hockey puck on ice slides a long distance after being struck. No continuous force is applied; it slows only due to friction (an external force). In ideal conditions with no friction, it would never stop.

problems.final_answer: The statement is incorrect because objects maintain motion without continuous force unless opposing forces act.

problems.problem: A ball is dropped in a smoothly moving train and in an accelerating train. Describe its motion in both cases and explain the difference using Newton's first law.

step_by_step.explanation: Ball drops straight down relative to the train. It inherits the train's horizontal velocity, and no horizontal forces act, so it maintains that velocity.

Ball appears to fall backward relative to the train because the train accelerates forward while the ball maintains its original horizontal velocity. No horizontal force acts on the ball, so its horizontal velocity remains constant (Newton's first law).

problems.final_answer: In constant-velocity train, ball drops straight down. In accelerating train, ball appears to fall backward due to inertia.

problems.problem: A 5 kg block rests on a 30° incline. The coefficient of static friction is 0.4. Will the block slide down?

step_by_step.explanation: $24.5\text{ N} > 16.97\text{ N}$, so the block will slide.

problems.final_answer: Yes, the block will slide down.

problems.problem: Explain why it is generally easier to keep an object moving than to start it moving. Include the relationship between static and kinetic friction coefficients in your explanation.

step_by_step.explanation: Static friction coefficient ($\mu_s$) is generally greater than kinetic friction coefficient ($\mu_k$).

Maximum static friction force ($\mu_s N$) is larger than kinetic friction force ($\mu_k N$). More force is required to overcome static friction and start motion than to overcome kinetic friction and maintain motion.

problems.final_answer: Easier to keep moving because $\mu_k < \mu_s$, so kinetic friction is less than maximum static friction.

problems.problem: A car traveling at 25 m/s approaches a 15° downhill slope. The coefficient of kinetic friction between tires and road is 0.7. The driver brakes hard, locking the wheels (kinetic friction only—no ABS). Find: the distance traveled before the car stops, and compare this to the stopping distance on level ground from the same initial speed.

step_by_step.explanation: Taking downhill as the positive direction. Friction opposes motion, acting uphill: $f_k = \mu_k mg\cos15° = 0.7 \times m \times 9.8 \times 0.9659 = 6.626m\text{ N}$.

problems.final_answer: Downhill stopping distance: 76.4 m; level ground: 45.6 m. Downhill stopping distance is longer because gravity reduces deceleration.

problems.problem: A 2 kg block rests on an incline where the angle θ can be varied. The coefficients are μₛ = 0.5 and μₖ = 0.35. The block is given a gentle push down the incline at θ = 25°. Find: (a) Does the block continue to accelerate, move at constant velocity, or stop? (b) Find the range of angles for which the block, once moving, will come to rest on its own. (c) Find the critical angle above which the block cannot remain at rest even without any push.

step_by_step.calculation:
$$F_{\parallel} = mg\sin25° = 2 \times 9.8 \times 0.4226 = 8.28\text{ N}$$
$$f_k = \mu_k mg\cos25° = 0.35 \times 2 \times 9.8 \times 0.9063 = 6.22\text{ N}$$
$$F_{\text{net}} = 8.28 - 6.22 = 2.06\text{ N down}$$
step_by_step.explanation: Net force is positive down, so block accelerates.

step_by_step.step: Angle for moving block to stop
step_by_step.explanation: Block stops if kinetic friction decelerates it to rest (assuming no additional pushes). Condition: $f_k > F_{\parallel}$ → $\mu_k mg\cos\theta > mg\sin\theta$ → $\mu_k > \tan\theta$ → $\theta < \tan^{-1}(0.35) = 19.3°$.

step_by_step.step: Critical angle for rest without push
step_by_step.explanation: Block remains at rest if $\mu_s \ge \tan\theta$ → $\theta_c = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.6°$.
step_by_step.calculation: Above 26.6°, block cannot remain at rest even without push.

problems.problem: A package is placed on a conveyor belt moving at 3 m/s. The belt suddenly decelerates at 2 m/s². The package is 5 kg with μₛ = 0.4 and μₖ = 0.3 with the belt. Find: (a) Does the package slip relative to the belt during deceleration? (b) If it doesn't slip, what is the friction force on the package? (c) If it does slip, how long until the package comes to rest relative to the ground?

step_by_step.explanation: Belt decelerates at 2 m/s². Since this required acceleration is less than the maximum possible acceleration from static friction (3.92 m/s²), the package does not slip.

problems.final_answer: (a) No slip; (b) 10 N static friction; (c) Not applicable.

problems.problem: A 15 kg box is pulled across a horizontal floor by a rope that makes an angle of 25° above the horizontal. The tension in the rope is 80 N. Find the normal force exerted by the floor on the box.

step_by_step.explanation: Forces in the vertical direction: weight ($mg$) downward, normal force ($N$) upward, and the vertical component of tension ($T\sin\theta$) upward. Since the box does not accelerate vertically, $\Sigma F_y = 0$.
Sum of upward forces equals sum of downward forces: $N + T_y = mg$

problems.problem: Two masses, m₁ = 3 kg and m₂ = 5 kg, are connected by a light string over a frictionless pulley. Find the acceleration of the masses and the tension in the string.
A 4.0 kg block on a frictionless horizontal table is connected by a light string over a pulley to a 2.0 kg hanging mass. Find the acceleration and tension.
A 5.0 kg block on a rough horizontal table (μₖ = 0.2) is connected by a light string over a pulley to a 3.0 kg hanging mass. Find the acceleration.

A mass m = 10 kg hangs from a movable pulley. A rope is attached to the ceiling, goes down to the movable pulley, up to a fixed pulley attached to the ceiling, then down to a hand pulling with force F = 60 N. All pulleys are ideal. Find the acceleration of the mass m.

step_by_step.explanation: For a single movable pulley, the rope supports the mass with two segments. Tension in each segment equals the pulling force F. Rope segments supporting the movable pulley: 2 → mechanical advantage = 2.
step_by_step.calculation:
$$T = F = 60\text{ N}$$
$$\text{Upward force on mass} = 2T = 120\text{ N}$$

problems.problem: A load of 500 N needs to be lifted using a single movable pulley with ideal pulleys and rope. What force must be applied to the free end of the rope to lift the load at constant velocity?

step_by_step.explanation: Single movable pulley provides mechanical advantage of 2.

problems.problem: A 5.0 kg block slides down a frictionless incline angled at 25° to the horizontal. Find: (a) The acceleration of the block; (b) The normal force exerted by the incline.
A 4.0 kg block rests on a 20° incline. The coefficient of static friction is 0.35. Will the block slide?

step_by_step.explanation: $13.41\text{ N} > 12.89\text{ N}$, so the block will slide.
